* * *

Answering Your Questions About Friedrich Nietzsche

## Transcript

Who was Friedrich Nietzsche?
Friedrich Nietzsche was a German philosopher and cultural critic who was fascinated by Western religion, morality, and philosophy during the 19th century. He is considered one of the most influential of all modern thinkers.
What is Nietzsche known for?
Friedrich Nietzsche is best known for devaluing traditional morality and religion, often in a struggle against nihilism—the idea that life has no inherent meaning. He also introduced key ideas like perspectivism (that there’s no single truth), the will to power (the human drive for self-determination), and the Übermensch—the ideal human of the future who rises above conventional knowledge and morality by creating his own.
What was Friedrich Nietzsche’s childhood like?
Nietzsche’s childhood was heavily influenced by the women in his life. His father, Carl Ludwig Nietzsche, died before his fifth birthday. He spent most of his early life in a household of five women: his mother, Franziska; his younger sister, Elisabeth; his maternal grandmother; and two aunts.
Where did Nietzsche study?
In 1864 Nietzsche attended the University of Bonn, concentrating on theology and classical philology—the study of classical languages and culture through the examination of ancient texts. After two less-than-successful semesters, he followed his scholarly mentor, Friedrich Wilhelm Ritschl, to the University of Leipzig, where he prospered.
What did Nietzsche mean by “God is dead”?
Nietzsche’s infamous pronouncement “God is dead” refers to the collapse of the religious, moral, and metaphysical absolutes that had formed the framework of Christian society throughout western Europe. This collapse was largely due to the rise of the empirical sciences. Without such absolutes, life had no transcendent meaning or purpose.
Was Nietzsche a fascist?
Despite strongly opposing nationalism, antisemitism, and power politics, Nietzsche’s works—particularly The Will to Power—were misinterpreted by fascists. After he fell ill late in his life, Nietzsche’s sister, who was a staunch antisemite, took over his estate and began heavily editing his writings to link him to the Nazi regime. This act changed the trajectory of Nietzsche’s legacy until the 1950s.
